payload.text:
**Investigating Vitest and tsconfig integration**

I'm piecing together how Vitest handles TypeScript configurations and types compared to Jest. It looks like Vitest relies more on esbuild for running tests, with type information managed through the `tsconfig.json` for IDE support rather than Jest's `tsconfig.test.json`. I'm considering the role of plugins like `vite-tsconfig-paths` and environment variables to ensure Vitest picks up the right TS config, especially when trying to include both Jest and Vitest types separately. I'll check the actual tsconfig files next to confirm these hypotheses.

summary.text:
**Investigating Vitest and tsconfig integration**

I'm piecing together how Vitest handles TypeScript configurations and types compared to Jest. It looks like Vitest relies more on esbuild for running tests, with type information managed through the `tsconfig.json` for IDE support rather than Jest's `tsconfig.test.json`. I'm considering the role of plugins like `vite-tsconfig-paths` and environment variables to ensure Vitest picks up the right TS config, especially when trying to include both Jest and Vitest types separately. I'll check the actual tsconfig files next to confirm these hypotheses.

payload.text:
**Investigating Vitest tsconfig usage**

I'm exploring how to make Vitest use the `tsconfig.test.json` for paths and type resolution, considering common plugins like `vite-tsconfig-paths` and how to conditionally load the test tsconfig in `vite.config` or a dedicated `vitest.config.ts`. I'm weighing options like merging configs, adjusting `esbuild.tsconfigRaw`, or changing plugin projects, while acknowledging some uncertainties about Vitest's native typechecking support and configuration APIs.

summary.text:
**Investigating Vitest tsconfig usage**

I'm exploring how to make Vitest use the `tsconfig.test.json` for paths and type resolution, considering common plugins like `vite-tsconfig-paths` and how to conditionally load the test tsconfig in `vite.config` or a dedicated `vitest.config.ts`. I'm weighing options like merging configs, adjusting `esbuild.tsconfigRaw`, or changing plugin projects, while acknowledging some uncertainties about Vitest's native typechecking support and configuration APIs.

{"output":"👋 Check out [Radashi](https://github.com/radashi-org/radashi), my latest endeavor. ---   # vite-tsconfig-paths [![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/vite-tsconfig-paths.svg)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/vite-tsconfig-paths) [![Code style: Prettier](https://img.shields.io/badge/code_style-prettier-ff69b4.svg)](https://github.com/prettier/prettier) Give [`vite`] the ability to resolve imports using TypeScript's path mapping. [`vite`]: https://github.com/vitejs/vite ## Usage 1. Install as dev dependency 2. Ensure the project either has `"type": "module"` set or that the Vite config is renamed to `vite.config.mjs` / `vite.config.mts` depending on whether TypeScript is used 3. Inject `vite-tsconfig-paths` in the Vite config ```ts import { defineConfig } from 'vite' import tsconfigPaths from 'vite-tsconfig-paths' export default defineConfig({ plugins: [tsconfigPaths()], }) ``` 4. (optional) ⚠️ To enable path resolution in non-TypeScript modules (e.g. `.vue`, `.svelte`, `.mdx`), you must set the `allowJs` option to true in your `tsconfig.json` file. If that doesn't work, you might need to enable `loose: true` to resolve all files. Note that, due to a Vite limitation, CSS files (and CSS dialects) cannot be resolved with this plugin (see [#30](https://github.com/aleclarson/vite-tsconfig-paths/issues/30)). **Note:** You need to restart Vite when you update your `paths` mappings. This is being tracked in [#17](https://github.com/aleclarson/vite-tsconfig-paths/issues/17) (contributions welcome). ### Options - `root: string` The directory to search for `tsconfig.json` files. The default value of this option depends on whether `projects` is defined. If it is, then the [Vite project root](https://vitejs.dev/config/shared-options.html#root) is used. Otherwise, Vite's [`searchForWorkspaceRoot`](https://vitejs.dev/guide/api-javascript.html#searchforworkspaceroot) function is used. - `projects: string[]` If you have an esoteric setup, you _might_ need this option to specify where your tsconfig files are located. The paths within are relative to the `root` option. If defined, the `root` directory won't be searched for tsconfig files. You should **always** try using just the `root` option first, because this option is more brittle. - `loose: boolean` Disable strictness that limits path resolution to TypeScript and JavaScript importers. Useful if you want imports in Vue templates to be resolved, but don't want to use `allowJs` in your tsconfig, for example. In other words, when `loose: true` is used, any file that gets transpiled into JavaScript will have its imports resolved by this plugin. - `parseNative: boolean` Enable use of the [`tsconfck.parseNative`](https://github.com/dominikg/tsconfck/blob/main/docs/api.md#parsenative) function, which delegates the loading of tsconfig files to the TypeScript compiler. You'll probably never need this, but I added it just in case. ⚠️ This option can slow down Vite's startup time by as much as 600ms, due to the size of the TypeScript compiler. Only use it when necessary. - `ignoreConfigErrors: boolean` When true, parsing errors encountered while loading tsconfig files will be ignored. This is useful if you have a monorepo with multiple tsconfig files, and you don't want to see errors for the ones that aren't relevant to the current project. - `skip: (dir: string) => boolean` A function that determines which directories to skip when searching for tsconfig.json files. While `.git` and `node_modules` directories are always skipped, this option allows you to skip additional directories, which is useful in large monorepos to improve performance.   ### allowJs If your tsconfig file has `"allowJs": true` in it, path resolution will be expanded beyond TypeScript importers. The following extensions will have their imports resolved by this plugin: `.vue`, `.svelte`, `.mdx`, `.mjs`, `.js`, `.jsx`   ### baseUrl If the `baseUrl` is defined, it gets prepended to all bare imports, and its resolution will take precedence over node_modules. This is also how TypeScript does it. Say the `baseUrl` is `../root` and you import `react`. This plugin will use `../root/react` if it exists. If not found, then `react` is resolved normally. The `baseUrl` is relative to the project root (where `tsconfig.json` lives).   ### include/exclude The `include` and `exclude` tsconfig options are respected. Internally, [globrex](https://github.com/terkelg/globrex) is used for glob matching.   ### Troubleshooting The `DEBUG` environment variable can be used to figure out why this plugin isn't working as you may have expected. ```sh DEBUG=vite-tsconfig-paths yarn vite ``` Also, check out the [Troubleshooting](https://github.com/aleclarson/vite-tsconfig-paths/wiki/Troubleshooting) wiki page for more guidance. ","metadata":{"exit_code":0,"duration_seconds":0.0}}

payload.text:
**Modifying tsconfigPaths plugin setup**

I’m updating the vite.config.mts to conditionally load tsconfig.test.json for Vitest by passing `{ projects: ['tsconfig.test.json'] }` to the tsconfigPaths plugin only in test mode; this should ensure test-specific TypeScript settings apply during testing. Next, I plan to implement this change using apply_patch and then recommend running Vitest to verify the setup works smoothly.

summary.text:
**Modifying tsconfigPaths plugin setup**

I’m updating the vite.config.mts to conditionally load tsconfig.test.json for Vitest by passing `{ projects: ['tsconfig.test.json'] }` to the tsconfigPaths plugin only in test mode; this should ensure test-specific TypeScript settings apply during testing. Next, I plan to implement this change using apply_patch and then recommend running Vitest to verify the setup works smoothly.

payload.name: apply_patch
payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: vite.config.mts
@@
-      tsconfigPaths(),
+      tsconfigPaths(
+        mode === 'test'
+          ? {
+              projects: ['tsconfig.test.json'],
+            }
+          : undefined
+      ),
*** End Patch

summary.text: **Preparing test run and file reference details**

payload.text:
**Summarizing tsconfigPaths update and test results**

I'm preparing a clear final summary explaining how the tsconfigPaths plugin now uses `tsconfig.test.json` in test mode, referencing line 66 in `vite.config.mts`. I'll include that running `yarn vitest run --passWithNoTests` passed with known warnings from dependencies like AnimateSharedLayout and Popper, noting these are existing issues. I'll highlight the change benefits Vitest’s type resolution without impacting other modes and decide whether to suggest any next steps or config tweaks.

payload.message:
- `vite.config.mts:66` now passes `{ projects: ['tsconfig.test.json'] }` to `vite-tsconfig-paths` when the Vite mode is `test`, so Vitest resolves imports (and any compiler options such as extra test-only `types`) using the dedicated `tsconfig.test.json` without affecting dev/build modes that continue to rely on the default tsconfig.
- Tests: `yarn vitest run --passWithNoTests` (all suites passed; Vitest only emitted the usual upstream warnings from Framer Motion/Popper during render).

summary.text:
**Summarizing tsconfigPaths update and test results**

I'm preparing a clear final summary explaining how the tsconfigPaths plugin now uses `tsconfig.test.json` in test mode, referencing line 66 in `vite.config.mts`. I'll include that running `yarn vitest run --passWithNoTests` passed with known warnings from dependencies like AnimateSharedLayout and Popper, noting these are existing issues. I'll highlight the change benefits Vitest’s type resolution without impacting other modes and decide whether to suggest any next steps or config tweaks.

content.text:
- `vite.config.mts:66` now passes `{ projects: ['tsconfig.test.json'] }` to `vite-tsconfig-paths` when the Vite mode is `test`, so Vitest resolves imports (and any compiler options such as extra test-only `types`) using the dedicated `tsconfig.test.json` without affecting dev/build modes that continue to rely on the default tsconfig.
- Tests: `yarn vitest run --passWithNoTests` (all suites passed; Vitest only emitted the usual upstream warnings from Framer Motion/Popper during render).
why is biome running twice on push? is husky using the pre-push hook and something in package.json that is duplicate?

payload.message: why is biome running twice on push? is husky using the pre-push hook and something in package.json that is duplicate?

payload.output:
{"output":"#!/bin/sh
. "$(dirname "$0")/_/husky.sh"

echo "Running `yarn lint`"
yarn lint
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "❌ `yarn lint` failed. Push cancelled."
  exit 1
fi

echo "Running `yarn build`"
yarn build
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "❌ `yarn build` failed. Push cancelled."
  exit 1
fi
","metadata":{"exit_code":0,"duration_seconds":0.0}}

{"output":"rg: No files were searched, which means ripgrep probably applied a filter you didn't expect.\nRunning with --debug will show why files are being skipped.\n","metadata":{"exit_code":2,"duration_seconds":0.1}}

{"output":"#!/bin/sh
if [ -z "$husky_skip_init" ]; then
  debug () {
    if [ "$HUSKY_DEBUG" = "1" ]; then
      echo "husky (debug) - $1"
    fi
  }

  readonly hook_name="$(basename "$0")"
  debug "starting $hook_name..."

  if [ "$HUSKY" = "0" ]; then
    debug "HUSKY env variable is set to 0, skipping hook"
    exit 0
  fi

  if [ -f ~/.huskyrc ]; then
    debug "sourcing ~/.huskyrc"
    . ~/.huskyrc
  fi

  export readonly husky_skip_init=1
  sh -e "$0" "$@"
  exitCode="$?"

  if [ $exitCode != 0 ]; then
    echo "husky - $hook_name hook exited with code $exitCode (error)"
  fi

  exit $exitCode
fi
","metadata":{"exit_code":0,"duration_seconds":0.0}}

        "noSelfCompare": "error",
        "noShadowRestrictedNames": "off",
        "noSparseArray": "error",
        "noTemplateCurlyInString": "error",
        "noUnsafeNegation": "error",
        "noUselessRegexBackrefs": "off",
        "noVar": "error",
        "noWith": "error",
        "useAdjacentOverloadSignatures": "off",
        "useAwait": "off",
        "useDefaultSwitchClauseLast": "off",
        "useGetterReturn": "error",
        "useGuardForIn": "error",
        "useIterableCallbackReturn": "off",
        "useNamespaceKeyword": "error"
      }
    }
  },
  "javascript": {
    "formatter": {
      "jsxQuoteStyle": "double",
      "quoteProperties": "asNeeded",
      "trailingCommas": "es5",
      "semicolons": "always",
      "arrowParentheses": "always",
      "bracketSameLine": false,
      "quoteStyle": "single",
      "attributePosition": "auto",
      "bracketSpacing": true
    }
  },
  "html": {
    "formatter": {
      "indentScriptAndStyle": false,
      "selfCloseVoidElements": "always"
    }
  },
  "overrides": [
    {
      "includes": ["**/*.{test,spec}.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"],
      "javascript": {
        "globals": [
          "describe",
          "it",
          "test",
          "expect",
          "beforeEach",
          "afterEach",
          "beforeAll",
          "afterAll",
          "jest",
          "vi"
        ]
      }
    },
    { "includes": ["src/ui/theme/**/*.ts"], "linter": { "rules": {} } },
    {
      "includes": ["*.test.ts", "*.test.tsx"],
      "linter": { "rules": { "suspicious": { "useAwait": "off" } } }
    },
    {
      "includes": ["*.stories.@(ts|tsx|js|jsx|mjs|cjs)", "*.story.@(ts|tsx|js|jsx|mjs|cjs)"],
      "linter": { "rules": {} }
    },
    {
      "includes": [".storybook/main.@(js|cjs|mjs|ts)"],
      "linter": { "rules": {} }
    },
    {
      "includes": ["*.ts", "*.tsx", "*.mts", "*.cts"],
      "linter": {
        "rules": {
          "complexity": { "noArguments": "error" },
          "correctness": {
            "noConstAssign": "off",
            "noGlobalObjectCalls": "off",
            "noInvalidConstructorSuper": "off",
            "noSetterReturn": "off",
            "noUndeclaredVariables": "off",
            "noUnreachable": "off",
            "noUnreachableSuper": "off",
            "useValidTypeof": "off"
          },
          "style": { "useConst": "error" },
          "suspicious": {
            "noDuplicateClassMembers": "off",
            "noDuplicateObjectKeys": "off",
            "noDuplicateParameters": "off",
            "noFunctionAssign": "off",
            "noImportAssign": "off",
            "noRedeclare": "off",
            "noUnsafeNegation": "off",
            "noVar": "error",
            "useGetterReturn": "off"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  ],
  "assist": {
    "enabled": true,
    "actions": { "source": { "organizeImports": "on" } }
  }
}

{"output":"
## Git Hooks

### Precommit

[lint-stages](https://github.com/okonet/lint-staged) will be automatically ran as a `precommit` hook ensuring that code is following our linting rules.

### Commit Messages

This project has been set up with [commitizen](https://github.com/commitizen/cz-cli).
We've decided as a team to stick with the conventions enforced by commitizen.
When creating a commit message, you can use `npx cz` to start a prompt that will guide you through these conventions.

## Technology References

This is a list of all major technologies used through the project. We will add some more in depth information as we go.

### Major Libraries

- [React](https://reactjs.org/)
- [Chakra UI](https://chakra-ui.com/)
- [React Query](https://react-query.tanstack.com/)
- [Zustand](https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand)
- [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios)
- [React Hook Form](https://react-hook-form.com/)
- [React Router](https://reactrouter.com/)

### Testing Technologies

- [Jest](https://jestjs.io/) and [ts-jest](https://www.npmjs.com/package/ts-jest)
- [React Testing Library](https://testing-library.com/)
- [Storybook](https://storybook.js.org/)
- [MSW](https://mswjs.io/)
  - we've removed from our Jest tests (you should mock `@state/*` hooks instead)
  - still active in Storybook, but we may want to remove it from there, too

### Build Tools

- [Vite](https://vitejs.org/)
- [Typescript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)
- biome (linting and formatting)
- [Husky](https://www.npmjs.com/package/husky)
  - pre-commit hook (lint-staged)
  - pre-push hook (tsc)
    - we have opted not to run tests do to slowness, but may revisit in the future
    - to override hooks for pushing test branches, use the `--no-verify` command

# Environments

| Environment | URL                                                      | Auth                                     |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Production  | [https://anedot.com](https://anedot.com)                 | Okta                                     |
| Sandbox     | [https://anedot-sandbox.com](https://anedot-sandbox.com) | provisioned                              |
| QA 1-10     | [https://qa1.anedot.com](https://qa1.anedot.com)         | provisioned & admin is same as local dev |
| Local       | [https://anedot.me:3000](https://anedot.me:3000)         | seeded admin                             |

## Logging

Logs are stored in [Graylog](https://logs.anedot.com) and error reporting is performed via [Rollbar](https://rollbar.com/anedot/all/items/?projects=31390).

payload.text:
**Explaining lint hook interactions and duplication**

I'll clarify how Biome runs in the hooks setup: pre-push runs `yarn lint` (Biome check) on the whole repo per `.husky/pre-push` lines 1-12, while `lint-staged` invokes `yarn lint:fix` (Biome) on staged files during pre-commit as configured on lines 268-270 in `package.json`. This explains why Biome might run twice—once when committing and once before pushing. I'll point out where these are defined and suggest how to avoid duplicate runs by adjusting or disabling hooks accordingly.

payload.message:
- Husky’s push hook is doing the first Biome run. `.husky/pre-push:1-15` shows the hook calls `yarn lint` before every push, and the `lint` script in `package.json:29` is simply `biome check`, so the entire codebase is linted once there.
- The second Biome run isn’t coming from another push hook entry in `package.json`; it comes from the pre‑commit tooling. The repo configures `lint-staged` (`package.json:268-270`) to execute `yarn lint:fix` (which is `biome check --write`) on staged files whenever the pre‑commit hook fires. That hook runs when you create the commit, so its Biome output often scrolls by right before you push.
- Put differently: you see Biome twice because Husky runs it at two different points (pre‑commit via `lint-staged`, then pre‑push via the explicit hook). If you only want one invocation, drop either the `lint` call from `.husky/pre-push` or the `lint-staged` hook, or use `HUSKY=0 git push`/`git push --no-verify` when you need to skip the push-time check.

summary.text:
**Explaining lint hook interactions and duplication**

I'll clarify how Biome runs in the hooks setup: pre-push runs `yarn lint` (Biome check) on the whole repo per `.husky/pre-push` lines 1-12, while `lint-staged` invokes `yarn lint:fix` (Biome) on staged files during pre-commit as configured on lines 268-270 in `package.json`. This explains why Biome might run twice—once when committing and once before pushing. I'll point out where these are defined and suggest how to avoid duplicate runs by adjusting or disabling hooks accordingly.

content.text:
- Husky’s push hook is doing the first Biome run. `.husky/pre-push:1-15` shows the hook calls `yarn lint` before every push, and the `lint` script in `package.json:29` is simply `biome check`, so the entire codebase is linted once there.
- The second Biome run isn’t coming from another push hook entry in `package.json`; it comes from the pre‑commit tooling. The repo configures `lint-staged` (`package.json:268-270`) to execute `yarn lint:fix` (which is `biome check --write`) on staged files whenever the pre‑commit hook fires. That hook runs when you create the commit, so its Biome output often scrolls by right before you push.
- Put differently: you see Biome twice because Husky runs it at two different points (pre‑commit via `lint-staged`, then pre‑push via the explicit hook). If you only want one invocation, drop either the `lint` call from `.husky/pre-push` or the `lint-staged` hook, or use `HUSKY=0 git push`/`git push --no-verify` when you need to skip the push-time check.
